evals.name: bitnami-upgrade-password-ignored
evals.prompt:
I'm upgrading a Bitnami PostgreSQL release and my connections are breaking after the upgrade. I have auth.postgresPassword set in my values file. Here's my upgrade command:

```bash
helm upgrade postgres0 bitnami/postgresql \
  --version 16.4.9 \
  --values postgres0_values.yaml \
  --namespace db \
  --atomic
```

Why are connections failing and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Bitnami charts auto-generate passwords on first install and store them in a Kubernetes Secret. On helm upgrade, the chart reads from that Secret and silently ignores any auth.postgresPassword in your values file — if the value differs from the stored Secret, the Secret is updated but the running database retains the old password, breaking all connections. Fix: retrieve the existing password from the Secret and pass it explicitly via --set auth.postgresPassword.
expectations: Explains that Bitnami charts store auto-generated passwords in a Kubernetes Secret and read from it on upgrade
Identifies that auth.postgresPassword in the values file is silently ignored during helm upgrade
Provides the kubectl command to retrieve the existing password: kubectl get secret postgres0 -n db -o jsonpath='{.data.postgres-password}' | base64 -d
Shows the corrected upgrade command passing the retrieved password via --set auth.postgresPassword
Avoids suggesting that simply re-setting the password in the values file will fix the issue

evals.name: atomic-install-failure-retry
evals.prompt: I ran `helm install postgres0 bitnami/postgresql --values postgres0_values.yaml --namespace db --atomic` and it failed partway through. Now I want to retry. Should I run `helm upgrade` or `helm install` again? Also, what does --atomic actually do on failure?
evals.expected_output: When --atomic is used and an install fails, Helm automatically rolls back and deletes the release entirely — there is no revision left in the namespace. Because the release no longer exists, you must re-run helm install (not helm upgrade). Additionally, every helm install and helm upgrade command must include --version to pin the chart version; omitting it silently pulls the latest chart, which may restructure values or rename keys.
expectations: States that --atomic on a failed install rolls back and deletes the release entirely (not just rolls back to a prior revision)
Correctly instructs to re-run helm install, not helm upgrade, because the release was deleted
Flags that the example command is missing --version and explains why pinning is required
Explains that omitting --version silently pulls the latest chart version, which may restructure values or rename keys

evals.name: custom-image-pull-secret-values
evals.prompt: I want to deploy a custom PostgreSQL image that extends the Bitnami base with the pgvector extension. My image is at rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/myorg/pgvector:17.3.0-debian-12-r0 and I have a registry pull secret named scaleway.registry in the db namespace. Write the values file section I need.
evals.expected_output: The values file needs global.security.allowInsecureImages: true because Bitnami enforces image signing and custom images fail that check. The imagePullSecrets entry must be a flat string list (- scaleway.registry), not a list of objects ({name: scaleway.registry}) which is the Kubernetes pod spec format. The image block should pin registry, repository, and tag exactly.
expectations: Includes global.security.allowInsecureImages: true and explains it is required because Bitnami enforces image signing for custom images
Formats imagePullSecrets as a flat string list (- scaleway.registry), not as Kubernetes pod spec objects ({name: scaleway.registry})
Sets image.registry, image.repository, and image.tag as separate fields matching the provided image reference
Pins the image tag to the exact provided tag (17.3.0-debian-12-r0) rather than using 'latest'
Avoids recommending the Kubernetes pod spec imagePullSecrets format ({name: ...}) which is incorrect for Bitnami chart values
